parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff adds 370+ lines of new retry/error-dispatch core logic (retry.mjs, bigmodel-errors.mjs) layered onto glm-client.mjs, but the integration surface is large and the deletion of process.mjs creates a dangling-import risk. The retry system's interaction with job tracking and state persistence under concurrent or partial-failure conditions is the highest-concern area; mis-categorized errors will either silently swallow failures or produce duplicate side-effect operations. The change should not ship until idempotency guards and import hygiene are verified end-to-end.

findings.title: process.mjs deleted — risk of dangling imports causing runtime crashes
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs shows 67 lines deleted and 0 added: the entire module was removed. The companion modules that most likely imported it — glm-companion.mjs (139 lines changed), render.mjs (79 lines changed), job-control.mjs (20 lines changed), tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) — were all modified in the same sweep, but the breadth of the change makes it plausible that a secondary consumer (e.g., a hook script, an indirect re-export, or a test helper) still references process.mjs. Any surviving `import … from './lib/process.mjs'` or `require` will throw at runtime with no opportunity for graceful degradation. The commit message 'Bundle F — ReferenceError + full thread/resume scaffolding purge' indicates this deletion was itself a bug-fix response to a ReferenceError, which raises confidence that the cleanup may still be incomplete.
findings.recommendation: Run `grep -r 'process\.mjs' scripts/ test-automation/ tests/` (and any other consumer directories) against the working tree. If any hit is found outside the already-updated files, add a redirect module or update the import before release.

findings.title: New retry layer lacks visible idempotency guards — duplicate job submissions on retriable errors
findings.body: retry.mjs (203 new lines) and bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 new lines) together form a retry/error-classification system that wraps glm-client.mjs API calls. For an LLM/AI job-submission client, the most common retriable errors (network timeout, 503, rate-limit 429) overlap with operations that create server-side state (job creation, task submission). If the client issues a non-idempotent POST, a retry after a timeout will produce a duplicate job — silent, invisible to the user, potentially incurring cost. The 352-line test file for retry suggests the retry logic itself is well-tested in isolation, but there is no evidence in the diff stat of idempotency-key injection, request deduplication, or 'check-then-create' guards in glm-client.mjs. The tracked-jobs.mjs changes (78 lines) compound this: if a retried submission succeeds twice, two job entries may be tracked, and subsequent status/cancel operations would be ambiguous.
findings.recommendation: Audit every call site where retry() wraps a non-idempotent glm-client operation (job creation, submission, cancellation). Ensure either (a) the operation is idempotent by design (e.g., idempotency key header), or (b) retry is only applied to read/status endpoints, or (c) tracked-jobs.mjs deduplicates by request fingerprint before recording a new job.

findings.title: Error misclassification in bigmodel-errors.mjs can silently swallow failures or cause retry storms
findings.body: bigmodel-errors.mjs (168 new lines) is the single decision point for whether an error is retriable, fatal, or requires special handling. If an error variant is unrecognized (e.g., a new BigModel API error code, a changed response schema, or an unexpected HTTP status), the default classification path determines behavior. A default-to-retry policy causes retry storms on novel errors; a default-to-fatal policy causes silent failures on transient but unrecognized conditions. Neither the test file (189 lines) nor the diff stat provides evidence of a default-branch audit or an exhaustion safeguard that logs and surfaces unrecognized error shapes. Under version skew (API changes between releases), this is a live risk.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the error classifier has an explicit default branch that (1) logs the full unrecognized error payload at warn/error level, (2) does NOT retry by default (fail-safe), and (3) includes the error shape in any user-facing message so it can be reported. Add a test case for a completely unknown error shape to verify this path.

findings.title: State persistence under concurrent retries — fs.mjs + state.mjs race window
findings.body: fs.mjs (44 lines changed) and state.mjs (32 lines changed) handle file-based state persistence. tracked-jobs.mjs (78 lines changed) reads and writes this state. When the new retry system kicks in, multiple concurrent retry timers may attempt to update state files simultaneously. Without file-locking or atomic write-and-rename, a partially-written state file can be read by a concurrent writer, leading to data loss (truncated JSON) or stale reads (missed job updates). The deletion of process.mjs (which may have contained concurrency primitives) and its replacement with retry.mjs (timer-based) widens this window because retries introduce async concurrency that was not previously possible.
findings.recommendation: Verify that fs.mjs uses atomic write (write-to-temp + rename) for state files. If state.mjs or tracked-jobs.mjs performs read-modify-write without serialization, add a simple advisory lock (e.g., lockfile with PID + timestamp) or serialize writes through a single async queue.

next_steps: Run a full-tree grep for 'process.mjs' references and confirm zero hits outside intentionally-updated files.
Trace every retry() call site in glm-client.mjs and classify each wrapped operation as idempotent or not; document the classification.
Verify bigmodel-errors.mjs has a safe default for unrecognized error shapes (fail-open vs fail-closed) and add a regression test for it.
Confirm fs.mjs uses atomic writes for all state file mutations; add a concurrent-write test if one does not exist.
Run the full UAT suite (v0.4.5 scenarios A–I) against a live or staging endpoint under simulated network flakiness to exercise the retry path end-to-end.
